children.name: Spratelloides gracilis
children.description: The silver-stripe round herring, slender sprat, or Kibinago minnow is a small, herring-like forage fish. They are small fish used as fishing bait, especially in skipjack tuna-fishing. It is valued as food in Japan, where it is known as kibinago.

children.name: Nematalosa nasus
children.description: The Bloch's gizzard shad (Nematalosa nasus), also known as gizzard shad, hairback, long-finned gizzard shad, long-ray bony bream and thread-finned gizzard shad, is a widespread and common, small to medium-sized anadromous fish found in all marine, freshwater and brackish waters throughout the Indo-West Pacific, from the Andaman Sea, South China Sea and the Philippines to the Korean peninsula.

children.name: Alosa agone
children.description: Alosa agone is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Alosa.

children.name: Alosa algeriensis
children.description: Alosa algeriensis, the North African shad, is a Mediterranean species of clupeid fish in the shad genus Alosa.

children.name: Alosa alosa
children.description: The allis shad is a widespread Northeast Atlantic species of fish in the Alosidae family. It is an anadromous fish which migrates into fresh water to spawn. It is found in the eastern Atlantic Ocean, the western Baltic Sea and the western Mediterranean Sea.

children.name: Alosa alosa x Alosa fallax
children.description: Alosa alosa x Alosa fallax — a species in the genus Alosa, family Clupeidae. Also known as herrings & sprats.

children.name: Alosa braschnikowi
children.description: Alosa braschnikowi, the Caspian marine shad or Brazhnikov's shad, is one of the alosid fish species endemic to the Caspian Sea.

children.name: Alosa caspia
children.description: Alosa caspia is a species of alosid fish, classified as one of the shad species (genus Alosa) endemic to the Caspian Sea basin.

children.name: Alosa kessleri
children.description: Alosa kessleri, also referred to as the Caspian anadromous shad, the blackback, or the black-spined herring, is a species of alosid fish.

children.name: Alosa killarnensis
children.description: Category:Fish described in 1916 Category:Critically endangered fish Category:Critically endangered biota of Europe Category:Taxa named by Charles Tate Regan.

children.name: Alosa macedonica
children.description: {{Speciesbox | image =Alosa_macedonica.jpg | status = CR | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = | taxon = Alosa macedonica | authority = (Vinciguerra, 1921) | synonyms = | synonyms_ref = }}.

children.name: Alosa mediocris
children.description: The hickory shad, fall herring, mattowacca, freshwater taylor or bonejack is a member of the family Alosidae, ranging along the East Coast of the United States from Florida to the Gulf of Maine. It is an anadromous fish species, meaning that it spawns in freshwater portions of rivers, but spends most of its life at sea. It is subject to fishing, both historic and current, but it is often confused with or simply grouped together with American shad in catch statistics.

children.name: Alosa sapidissima
children.description: The American shad is a species of anadromous clupeid fish naturally distributed on the North American coast of the North Atlantic, from Newfoundland to Florida, and as an introduced species on the North Pacific coast. The American shad is not closely related to the other North American shads. Rather, it seems to form a lineage that diverged from a common ancestor of the European taxa before these diversified.

children.name: Alosa saposchnikowii
children.description: Alosa saposchnikowii, or the saposhnikovi shad, is a species of fish in the clupeid genus Alosa.

children.description: {{Speciesbox | image = Amblygaster clupeoides.png | image_caption = | taxon = Amblygaster clupeoides | authority = Bleeker, 1849 | synonyms = | synonyms_ref = }} Amblygaster clupeoides, the Bleeker smoothbelly sardinella, blue pilchard, sharp-nosed pilchard, or sharpnose sardine, is a reef-associated marine species of sardinellas in the herring family Clupeidae.

children.name: Sardinella
children.description: Sardinella — a genus of herrings & sprats in the family Clupeidae.

children.name: Sardinella albella
children.description: The white sardinella (Sardinella albella), also known as deep-bodied sardine, perforated-scale sardine or short-bodied sardine, is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella atricauda
children.description: Sardinella atricauda (Bleeker's blacktip sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella from the western Pacific.

children.name: Sardinella brachysoma
children.description: Sardinella brachysoma (deepbody sardinella, Indian herring, or Indian sprat) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella brasiliensis
children.description: Sardinella brasiliensis, (Brazilian sardinella or orangespot sardine) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella fimbriata
children.description: Sardinella fimbriata (fringescale sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella goni
children.description: Sardinella goni — a species in the genus Sardinella, family Clupeidae. Also known as herrings & sprats.

children.name: Sardinella hualiensis
children.description: Sardinella hualiensis (Taiwan sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella jussieu
children.description: Sardinella jussieu (Mauritian sardinella or goldstripe sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella lemuru
children.description: Sardinella lemuru (Bali sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella found in the Eastern Indian Ocean and in the Western Pacific Ocean, in the area that stretches from southern Japan through the Malay Archipelago to the western Australia.

children.name: Sardinella maderensis
children.description: thumb|Global capture production of Madeiran sardinella (Sardinella maderensis) in thousand tonnes from 1970 to 2022, as reported by the [[Food and Agriculture Organization|FAO ]] Sardinella maderensis (Madeiran sardinella) is a species of small ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella which is found in the Eastern Atlantic and Southeastern Mediterranean.

children.name: Sardinella melanura
children.description: Sardinella melanura (blacktip sardinella) is a species of ray-finned fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinella pacifica
children.description: Sardinella pacifica is a species of marine fish of the sardines in the family Clupeidae belonging to the genus Sardinella, which is endemic to the waters around the Philippines.

children.name: Sardinella sindensis
children.description: The Sind sardinella (Sardinella sindensis), often referred to as sardines, is a species of ray-finned schooling fish in the genus Sardinella.

children.name: Sardinops sagax
children.description:
Sardinops is a genus of sardines of the family Alosidae. Often considered monotypic with only member as Sardinops sagax, it currently has three valid species:Sardinops melanosticta (Temminck & Schlegel, 1846) 
Sardinops ocellatus (Pappe, 1853) 
Sardinops sagax (Jenyns, 1842)
Sardinops sagax caeruleus (Girard, 1854) 
Sardinops sagax musica (Girard, 1855)
Sardinops sagax neopilchardus (Steindachner, 1879)

children.name: Sardina pilchardus
children.description: The European pilchard is a species of ray-finned fish in the monotypic genus Sardina. The young of the species are among the many fish that are sometimes called sardines. This common species is found in the northeast Atlantic, the Mediterranean, and the Black Sea at depths of 10–100 m (33–328 ft).

children.name: Clupea
children.description: Clupea — a genus of herrings & sprats in the family Clupeidae.

children.name: Clupea pallasii
children.description: The Pacific herring is a species of the herring family associated with the Pacific Ocean environment of North America and northeast Asia. It is a silvery fish with unspined fins and a deeply forked caudal fin. The distribution is widely along the California coast from Baja California north to Alaska and the Bering Sea; in Asia, the distribution is south to Japan, Korea, and China.
